speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This second conversation with George Schmaltz took place at his home in Lewiston Orchards on August 27, 1976. The interviewer was Sam Schrager.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: A couple of 300 feet above every creek where the bushes were laying on the ground. They were just down with berries.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: They'd been like that once since Mrs. Vine told me. That was 1946. The summer of 1946 that the bushes laid on the ground.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: One year since that came up here they told me that they were just like, did I say 1946?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: No, I mean, 1926. And one year after that they'd been like that. And this Mrs. Olsen that I got my berries from, she said that the bushes were just loaded.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This year?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Seems like Mrs. Vine told me that the man that runs the store at Elk River, he also delivers groceries to, you know, the fire protective associations got a substation instead of bowls, cabins. They must have built some kind of an outfit in there. And he delivers groceries to them. And he said the huckleberries are just thicker than the dickens up there where he goes to deliver groceries. But he might be doing it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever hear of the guy that they called the Ridge Runner?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Yeah, I know him.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Bill Morland. That was his name. Oh, yeah. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did you know him?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: No, but I've heard about him. I've heard he was quite a character. Oh, yeah, I know him. He was a little fellow. Oh, yeah. I had to laugh. When I was on civil service with the Bureau of Entomology, we worked right in with the Forest Service. We did work for the Forest Service. We had our separate setup in the Forest Service. Only they used our headquarters a lot. We had that roughly headquarters at Clarkia. And one fellow there, a senior, Merle Lokes, he was on Forest Service, Mr. Rust. And the Ridge Runner, he made a habit to clean out the ... ... ... ... No.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Round Table. Round Table. He cleaned out Round Table. And this time, they spotted his tracks. Merle Lokes told me this himself. He told us from the horse's mouth. Well, Merle Oaks and two other guys, I forget their name, I didn't know them, that's why I left, they tracked him for miles and miles. And finally they came to this meadow in the woods. And his tracks ended right in the middle of the meadow. And Merle was like, by God's word, I was getting spooky. I started to look up in the trees.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know what he'd done?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: He walked backwards in his tracks, till he got to a stream that he had crossed. And then he walked up in the water, in the stream. And that's how he lost the tracks. And they'd been catching that time. But that's how it happened. And his tracks ended, my God, Merle said, I was getting spooky.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: He wasn't a bad fella. He was a good guy.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: My daughter, she works in that drugstore up on the hill, you know, that old drugstore up on Norman Hill. And she had a friend, I wonder if Ferdinand.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Her husband was a cedar pole man.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Johnson, I think his name was. I forget her first name. Well anyway, this Johnson woman, she worked up at headquarters, seemed like she worked in a drugstore. And she got real well acquainted with Bill Morland. Boy, I tell you, you didn't dare to say anything against him to her. Right away, she got kind of a chip on your shoulder if you said anything about Bill Morland.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did she think of him?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: I mean, what they just talked, he and her were friends. Yeah, they were friends, just talked, you know.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: He'd come into the drugstore and buy stuff, and she'd talk to him. He said he was just as nice a fella as she ever saw.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what did they have against him anyway?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Was it that he was just stealing from the... Stealing from the Forest Service. That's all they had against him. They didn't have anything against him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did he take? Was it mostly food?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Yeah, all he got was food. You know. That's all. He just lived there. And when they caught him, they thought maybe he was an escaped criminal from someplace, but they couldn't find anything against him, you know. And one year before I got on steady with the Civil Service, I worked for a while in the spring for the Potliff Timber Protective Association at Elk River, and he worked there too then. See, that's how I got to know him. Oh, you're just a little fella. He never bothered nobody.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Have you ever read that book about him? Burt Russell? Huh?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: There was a story by Burt Russell about him. Oh, there's a whole book about him.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: There's a little book about him written by a retired, I think he was super wiser for the Clearwater Forest.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that Space?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Yes. He wrote the book. I think you can buy it at Orofino from Space.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I think he lives at Orofino, don't he?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: I think so, yeah. Yeah, you can get that book, I'm sure, from him. It's a really interesting book. You know, he lived in the hollow cedar one whole winter.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Can you imagine that?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Yeah. Bill Morland, the original. A friend of mine, after they caught him and had him in jail in Orofino, a friend of mine, he's also a blister ruster by AMSPAW, went and talked to him. They had quite a talk there in jail. I told him, my God, you ought to write a book.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Bill Morland. You can get that book, I'm sure, from Space. Mrs. Johnson, my daughter's son, she had the book. And I read it. She lent it to me and I read it.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: I've been asking what the bootlegging was like in Elk River during Prohibition. You know, I understood from what people told me out there that there was, like there was an Austrian woman that was doing a lot of selling of moonshine.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Yeah. You know, I don't know, there's a fellow by the name of Hunt that did some bootlegging. He didn't do any moonshine.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: And I wasn't even with that crowd too much, because my interest was hunting and fishing and trapping. I trapped every Sunday in the wintertime, all through the winter, you know. I made, well, I'll tell you, you know, you had to put in a yearly report to Boise how much you catch, for this word. And you know, one winter I made $351. In the next winter I made $352. And the third winter I made $353.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Can you beat that?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: But then, the fourth winter, I think I only made around $150. I had all the mink cleaned out. See, the mink was, that was my money. But I had the mink all cleaned out, and I don't remember, but it was way down.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did you catch him?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Well, mostly I used hollow logs. I'd find hollow cedars, you know, you know, about so big around, and with a hollow I'd walk like that, and I'd cut off pieces like so, and I'd nail one end shut, and I'd set my trap inside the log. Of course, in Elk River, you know, there's a lot of snow, you know, in the winter. And you can set your traps out in the open, you know.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: And then I used timber bearing leaves for trap coverage. And then I had to protest against the log, but I used to turn away in rough -grow season. I used to cut the fine feathers off with a pair of scissors, and I had a little bag with a straw, a drawstring, and I'd scatter a few feathers down towards the water, and hail, and the mink smelled of. And then, of course, I had bait inside the log, too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What season is it? What season was that? The rough, do you call that rough -grow?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Yeah, you know, you're a... We call them woods, type of. Oh, yeah.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Oh, that reminds me. We have a drow in Sweden that's almost as big as the curtain. It goes up to 15 pounds, you know. And I... I had a... When I was running headquarters there for the deer event tomorrow, during the wintertime, the supervisor used to come up, and so much acting, we'd sit and shoot the bull, you know, and I told him about this big row that we had in Sweden. I'd weigh up to 15 pounds. The English name is Caper Kelly. And I couldn't imagine worrying the hell they got that name. The Swedes call him Chedr, and the Norwegians call him Chedr, and the Finns call him Chedra. But worrying the hell they got the name Caper Kelly, I don't know. So I looked it up in a dictionary. And there's some big woods in Scotland by the name of Caper Kelly Woods, where they used to have these birds, and they finally got extinct, and they went to the Scandinavian countries and got new breeding stouts. So they... In our English dictionary, they call them Caper Kelly, on account of the English name for them. But I was asking, tell you about Hartmann. And I'll tell him about this 15 -pound grouse, you know, and sit and look at that. And I finally asked him, God damn it, Hartmann.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I said, don't you believe what I'm telling you?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: I got a picture of one right in my scrapbook, with my face in the lower corner of the picture. And I said, my brother's double -barre shotgun is hanging above the grouse, and you can see both ends of the shotgun, but you can't see the wing feathers of the grouse. My mother was a photographer by trade before she married dad. She had her own studio. And she put a sheet up on the warehouse and took my picture. I still got it right in my scrapbook. Well, by God, I'd like to see it. So I had an extra picture that I brought with me next time I went home and I saw it. And by God, he said, I never heard of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Have you?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: No, that's the first time I've heard of it.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Dad, a 15 -pounder.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: 15 -pound grouse, huh?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Wait a minute. Wait a minute. Now, I think I saved that out a long time ago. And he worked a lot with those Swedes in the lumberyard, Harry's lumberyard. You probably heard old man Harry.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: And he just had a hang -up on getting those imported here. He never made it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Kentucky Johnson, huh?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Yeah, his name was Levi Johnson. And he knew all about it from talking to the Swedes in the lumberyard. But they're all over Sweden, you know, those big grouse. Well, I knew a Laplander.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: I used to be on the... When I got out of school, I got on with the Hydrographic Bureau over in Sweden. And we were in Lapland, measuring streams for, you know, for water power. And I knew a Laplander at one winter alone. He killed over 100 of those. See, you can sense a game there, you know. And he killed over 100. But the mouse was a solid bullet. It just makes a good hole, you know, clean hole.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, these mink that you trapped, were they an easy animal trap or were they smart?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Well, they're smarter than hell.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: I'll tell you, there's only one animal that I have caught. And I never was any good at catching coyotes. I could catch coyotes before snow came. But after snow, I would...

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: They're damn near, minky, damn near, smarter than coyote. Damn near. Not quite. And Martin would step into anything. You don't even have to cover your trap for Martin. But mink, oh. And I got good at it. I got to be a good mink tapper. But I worked at it. I got to be a good mink tapper. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did it get rough in the winter to get around and check the traps with all the heavy snow?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Well, I had Penobscot specialist snowshoes. They're them long ones, you know. And then after a while, I started to use skiis. Because I was an old skier from way back, you know. So I started to use skiis.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Yeah. You know, I was going to tell you the story. I don't know if you heard it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Have you ever heard about the Jericho Mine up at Elk River?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Just the name. I've heard the name.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Well, you know, long time ago, there never was any Jericho Mine. What they found was a great big boulder. What the miners called a float. It had come from someplace else. And in the passage there, I imagine by the ice age.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Well, they took, hi. Hey, what's your... Okay.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Are you recording?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: They took out what I heard, they took out $80 ,000 out of this boulder. An old Pete Charvie told a friend of mine about it. You heard of old Pete Charvie that had a mine down in Long Meadow Creek. No. Well, he was an old prospector in that country in the early days. He said, yes, this Christ was that boulder ever reached.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And yes, this Christ, did we ever scratch up that mountain?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Well, there... I guess somebody, I don't know who it was, that started a mine. And dug a tunnel up there. And then there was this Charlie Shields. He had a homestead about half ways between Jericho and the Clearwater River. Up in that kind of a flat country. You've been through that probably. And when he sold out to the timber, what timber company he sold to, I think he sold to the Potlatch . I understand he got $16 ,000 for it. And he spent that trying to find that vein. And of course, he had to do assessment work, you know. Well, then there was a crook from Leavenworth , Washington. The name was Lost Camp. He had been some kind of a businessman at Leavenworth , but I guess he was so damn crooked that his family and the town, I think, kicked him out. And he came and took up a claim below Charlie Shields. And then he befriended Charlie Shields. And he was doing the First World War. And Lost Camp said, when the war started, I don't know if it's Charlie. You know, nobody's going to come up here and check on our assessment work or anything. We don't have to do any assessment work. They just had a good time and Hunt didn't face the whole summer, you know.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: And I don't know if that has just went on one summer or two summers. But it seems like Charlie Shields had some friends out on the coast. And I don't know if it was just one summer that they didn't do any assessment work or if it was two summers. But anyway, Lost Camp hurried up and staked the claim on the mine and went to Orofino  and had it recorded and everything. And when Charlie Shields came back from the coast, he didn't have no mine. And he just stole it from Charlie Shields. Charlie Shields was a nice fella.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How could he get away with that? Because Charlie Shields hadn't done the assessment?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: See?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: He hadn't done any assessment work. So Lost Camp. So he just told Charlie Shields to get the hell out of there when he came. Nice guy. Oh yeah, hell of a nice fella. Yeah. Yeah. But he damn near went crazy trying to find that and leave and he never found nothing.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: He, well, he must have stayed there too. The Second World War, I think. He worked that mine from the First World War to the Second World War and he never found that thing. That's what he got for being crooked. Whatever became a charge of Shields, I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did people think of Lost Camp? Did he have many friends around?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: No. No.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: No. He didn't. No.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: He's, uh... Boy, if I had Charlie Shields, I could see knocking him off or something like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: I'm afraid that's what's going to happen. I couldn't have told who shot him out of the brush.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Huh?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Yeah. Well, I'm afraid that's just what's going to happen.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Hey, did I ask you about, when I was here before, did I ask you about this story of Big Red?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: I don't know if that was before you come into the country or not, but there was a big red who got killed near Elk River. Things get lost, forgotten. Well, I had the idea that those guys, a lot of those Yugoslavians, Serbian, Austrians, were kind of a separate group. They were more to themselves and didn't, you know, were considered separate from the other. Yeah. They were clannish, kind of.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: And a lot of them hung around their prostitutes, a lot of them.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Well, I wouldn't doubt it. Sam Pivar, she was kind of a shady character.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many sporting houses were there around Elk River?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Two. At the time. Two. That's about as many as there were around Bovill, too, I think. They were a couple.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Either one or two in Bovill. You know, as you drive into Elk River, you know, you go before you cross the last hill. You know, you go past a couple of farmhouses. Well, sporting houses were just a little past the last house that you pass before you go over the hill. They were just about, well, where that highway goes now, I'd say there'd be one on each side of the highway. And the old railroad track went riding, no, no, let's see now. No. The railroad track went west of them, past the, they used to call it the big whorehouse and the little whorehouse. One was bigger than the other. And the railroad track was just west of them, just, well, only about as far from here to those lilac bushes, to the logon tracks. That's where those guys I told you about. Sonny, so Coyote Smith. Coyote Smith and Sonny Nelson, yeah.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Were those girls in those houses, I'm curious about them, you know, what they were like as people.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they like mostly young or were they old women in a place?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Generally the landlady, their mama, she was generally middle age or so, but the whores were generally all the way from 20 to 40, I guess, something like that. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Could they just go into town, into Elk River, like anybody else, or were they kind of shunned by the town?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: They were shunned by the town people, yeah. Oh yeah, they were shunned by the town people. By the God I tell you, I don't know how they got their grub. The Dreymen must have hauled their grub out to them, because I've never seen them come into stores in town.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: I bet you, I bet you the Draymen hauled a grub out to them. They didn't have a hunger around town at all. They didn't even come into town.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Must have been kind of a lonely life in a way then. I mean, all they would have, I suppose, was the other whores, you know, for friends.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Yeah, that's all. That's all. Yeah.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: One fella that I knew real well, he married one of them, and they went to Spokane and run a hotel, and for years they had a restaurant in Spokane. They're both dead now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did, I wonder how, did he, you think he just fell in love with the girl?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: No, I don't think so. I think for one thing, I think he was over -sexed. And that did it. I don't think he actually fell in love with her.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: I wonder, I kind of wonder why, you know, what would make a, I kind of wonder what would make girls, you know, turn to that kind of life, if they'd just get desperate for money.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: I think most of them, no, I think most of them are just, they're over -sexed, and that's how they get started. I think so. That's what I think.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: I suppose it for lumberjacks, though, that was a pretty, it was probably for a lot of these guys, you know, not being married or anything, I think. Oh, that was a good thing to go in.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Oh, yeah. Yeah.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: I want to say that the lumberjack gets together and logs in the whorehouse, and when they get back to camp, they talk about the whorehouse.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Yeah, I never had any use for that loss camp.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Loss camp, yeah.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: I had a laugh one time. There was three of us going to go over to Big Island. There was Ed Cookenbaker and Earl Hood and Marcel. And we got all ready the day before.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Big Island.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where was that? Isn't that down on the Clearwater?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Yeah, right above where Swamp Creek comes in, because there's no island anymore. It's all Droschach, the rest of all now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now, what happened? You were going to go down and?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Go deer hunting in the fall. Weather about like today. And Ed had been playing poker all night. So we got up to the grave.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know where the grave we are, at East Upadance River? Up on the Hylbog now?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: I've seen it. I've gone there. Well, we got as far as the graveyard. And Ed said, by God, boys, I can't make it. I'll go home and go to sleep and rest, and I'll come out tomorrow. Okay, that was fine.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: So, Earl and I, we took off. And we got to the Jericho mine about noon. And the miners were all down in the shaft working. That was when Charlie Shales had it.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: And we rested a little while and had a little lunch. And then we got to take an inventory. And you know, Ed had everything that had any grease. He had all the lard. He had all the bacon. There wasn't a thing that we could have even fried an egg with, you know. It just happened that that was all in Ed's pack sack. But here the miners had seven or eight slabs of bacon hanging up on a ridge pole. You know, outside, you know where there's poaches built out, you know, like they used to do. Well, I'll tell you, Earl, I said, we can take a slab of bacon and we leave him a quart of whiskey. Well, Ed and L Well, Ed and Ladd was a quart of whiskey those days, you know, that was quite something, you see.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Clearwater county was dry, you know. The only way you could get whiskey is to send it to Spokane and have it come in by express. That's the only way you could get whiskey.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: By train?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That was legal?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Yeah, Mr. Sherry's been catching you between the deep one, you're home. Well, so we took a piece of paper and stuck it over the neck of the bottle and we drove compliments of Earl Hood and George Smalls.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: And by God, two days afterwards, Ed came. He came out. All right. And we hunted around there for, we must have hunted around for over a week down there. We stayed at the home fetters' cabin. Steve Willer was his name. And him and his wife, Myrtle, lived there, out there on the home street, on a bench above the river. Well, when we went home,

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: the miners had just come in from work in the evening when we hit the mine on our way back. And oh my God, they just fell all over themselves. Oh my God, that quarter whiskey that we left. Oh, they thanked us and thanked us. Finally, I said, well, I figured it was about an even break between the whiskey and the slab of bacon we took. And they never missed the bacon.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What bacon?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Well, we didn't have anything to fry anything with, so we had to have bacon. So we left you the whiskey. Well, by God, they were sure tickled to death. They just had a feast on that quarter whiskey, you know, in the middle of the week, too.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Yeah.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: But I, we were, there was four of us. No, I see there were Elmer Johnson and Bert Johnson and Dave and Harold. And I was five of us, staying in a cabin, oh, just about a half a mile below Big Island. It was used to call Shorty Green's cabin. And we had to go 14 miles to the dance to get our grub. So about, oh, once every other week or so, we'd go down to a dance. And there was an old Dutchman by the name of Chesley who had a little grocery store there at dance. And we used to go down there. And of course, I knew Steve and Myrtle Willard. And there was a homesteader lived about two miles below us on the river. Tom Howard had a cabin right up from the riverbank. Well, we stopped in to give Howard some stuff that he sent for us to get. Steve was there and he acted nervous as the dick and boy, he was nervous. And I noted there was blood all over his, his hunting knife, you know.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Because they didn't know us too well. They knew me, but they didn't know them other boys too well. So we said goodbye and started for home. We hadn't gone just a little ways. There was about eight inches of snow down by the river. And here came Myrtle Willard with a deer strap on each side of the horse. And because she didn't know the boys, the only one she knew was me, you know. And I was the last one. And here these guys come trotting along, you know, and she just pulled the horse off the side of the road and just stood there. She was scared to death until she said, oh, hello horse. And she knew everything was all right. I said, hello Myrtle.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How are you?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: And she just went on with it too there on the back of us.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Well, I can't believe that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I mean, people didn't pay no attention to the sea, to what was in season in those days, did they?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: No, no, no. No. No. We never paid no attention to the season. Yes, we did pay a little, but the homesteaders didn't. They got it whenever they could, and by God they were entitled to it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you mean the hunters like you guys from town? You would pay?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: They paid a little attention to the seas, the town guys. Except during the Depression, we never paid any attention to the sea. We, in fact, lived off of Venice during the Depression. But in those days, the town guys generally paid attention to hunting season, but the homesteaders didn't. They hunted year round.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: I'm surprised that the guys, you know, the town people did, but I guess that's just because it seems to me there's so much, there used to be so much game around there that, you know, I was thinking.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: There was more game now than there was then. More deer, or yolks, and more elk. There wasn't any elk around there then. No elk. The elk were imported. They were imported in the 1920s. They imported 40, head to Bogle. That's how they, they used to be elk in that country.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: There had been elk?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Oh yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: One time?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Oh yeah. But they were all killed off. And then they imported them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Killed off by the early homesteaders?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever know anybody who'd get caught in those days for shooting out of season?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: No. No, don't. I don't think they were looking too hard. No. Well, I'll tell you what happened. See, that fall, when I was down on the river with Earl Hood and Ed Cookenbaker, there was a game warden by the name of Snyder from Orofino , and he was going up the river further up Brown Bowl's cabin. He said there was a bunch of guys from Genesee that were dogging with deer, with dogs. That was an unforgivable sin. You could shoot deer out of season, but by God, don't get caught dogging them. That was, so I took care of his horse while he rode as far as Big Island, and then he took a parrot across. There was a little parrot there where you could pull across, and then he went up the river. And he never caught these guys, so when he come back, we just shook hands, and there was all those two of them. Well, then that winter, when I was shacking up with these guys in Schodi Green's cabin, when I told you we had to go down there and figure out where it was. The cabin was built on a kind of a side hill, and under the porch there was a little door where you could go in, and there was just room enough to hang a deer under there.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: And there was a fence, and the trail up the river was right on the other side of the fence. And I was right on the porch looking, and I seen this guy coming up the trail. So I walked over to the gate to see who he was. I got here for Snyder. He'd had to report on some more dogging going on up the river, and he had two air dales with him. Those goddamn air dales just started round. That goddamn cabin with a nose right to the bottom logs, just sniffing and sniffing and sniffing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And Snyder and I, we talked a while, and he'd say, God damn it, you know what he says?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: You know them goddamn dogs are always trying to look up something I don't want to see. Well, I couldn't say yes, and I couldn't say no. And what you said in case like that, I don't know what in the hell I said.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So what, he just went on?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Oh yeah, he just went on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But when he was by the first time checking on them guys, don't you think he might have had the idea that you guys were hunting?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Well, it was season then. Oh, it was season. Oh yeah, it was in season. But these Genesee guys used to have hounds, and they used to go up in that country and dog them, you know. But the second time, that wasn't season no more. No, no, that was in the middle of the winter. Seasonal stoves. In fact, it was long,

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: in February or March. Oh no, seasonal stoves then.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: But what he had known, that what you had there was out of season, that you had shot it out of season, even if the dogs, even if he looked. Oh yeah, I see. Because it wouldn't have kept.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: He knew that we had Vanish from hanging under there. He's gonna be crying. Yeah.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Yeah.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Oh.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: One time, we were gonna make some snowshoes. And you know, you can get the hair off a deer hide by laying it in the riffles, you know. You can weigh it down with stones in riffles, and every place along the hair will flip. You know, we were gonna make row hide.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Water riffles now?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Over the water. Oh yeah, okay. And the river just had a gradual slope from where we lived in that cabin. And it was in the spring, when the golden eagles were coming back, you know. And here came this golden eagle up the river. And the way it looked to me, it looked just like he was hitting right for me. But he was just, he was just following the same, because I was up here, and he was just following the same course, you know. And you know, when he went over me, his wings went... He was just that close. I just almost ducked, you know. They're big. Huge, yeah, they're big birds. They're bigger than the bald eagle. Way bigger.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Back in the old country, they feed on these big growls I was telling you about.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The eagles do?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Oh yes, the golden eagles. That's their meat. They feed on those big growls just the same as the red -tailed hawk feed on, feed some blue -growls here. You know, a red -tailed hawk will just stay with a covey of blue -growls till they're only one or two left. And they'll move on and find another batch.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Will they kill them all at once, or one at a time?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: No, that's the need of them.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Stay with them too. If they can't find any more, and then they move on, they can't find another covey. Yeah.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Yeah, there they go, the eagles and meat back in the old country.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Big growls.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what do you think the deal was with the game wardens in those days?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Because I've heard the same thing. Like what you're saying, that the game wardens, cases where the game warden would come and know, you know, that there was something. It was out of season, and we'd just ignore it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that just because the game wardens knew the people at that time, knew who the people were?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Yeah, yeah, I think so. I think so. Because I don't think any more they'd do that. No, oh no, no. No.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: One time there's a homesteader out there by Big Island. We used to call that country, the Big Island country. Red Nelson.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: And it must have been about eight inches or so. And the deer season was open, but the fashion season was closed. You know, the rough grouse season. And Earl Hood and I were going to go down and have dinner with Red Nelson.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: And I don't remember if I had three or four rough grouse. Because I didn't know who'd be dying down in the cabin. So I just threw their rough grouse in the snow by the gate, and then we walked down to the cabin.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why ain't God one fifteen, twenty minutes here the game warden came down to eat dinner with us?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: He asked us who those rough grouse belonged to that were laying up by the gate. God, we didn't know. No, we didn't know. He didn't press it either, but we left before he did, and we took the grouse with us. Nothing but a third of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were you doing spending the whole winter in that little cabin? What was the idea?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Everything was closed down up at Elk River.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This was depression?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: No, no.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This was before, huh?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Too much snow.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So guys would just take off and hang out in some little place like that?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: A lot of them.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: They used to call their shacking up. They'd go to an old homestead cabin that'd be pretty good shape, you know, and stay over winter.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that fun to do that?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Oh, yeah, it was fun. You'd put out rabbit snares, you know, and you'd shoot a deer out of season, you know. And, or we had a fella with us. He was born and raised in Michigan. His name was Bert Johnson. His mother was Swede and his father was Irish. And I'll tell you, he could make the best bacon father biscuits I've ever ate. And every afternoon, we had bacon father biscuits and butter and jam and coffee. And you know, that makes a good lunch.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: I was always a black sheep when he come to cookin'. I never learned to cook. Well, we'll say, well, we better fill up today because he's just turned to cook tomorrow.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you take turns cooking? Is that how you done it? One guy cooked every day?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Cook a day a piece. I cooked a day and Bert cooked the next day and Dave cooked the next day and so on. But Bert still cooked every day. He made the biscuits all the time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you guys get on each other's nerves after a while when you stay in the cabin together like that all through the winter?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: No, we never did.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: We were always doin' something. You know, there was awful good fishing. Just below where Swamp Creek ran out into the river, there was a deep hole and there was a raft, a log raft. And we used to pull it up along the shore and then pull out into the riffles and let it drift into this deep hole for a waste. And then we'd throw this rope with a rock on it down and there we were. Boy, I tell you, we caught fish over in macro.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were they, must they?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Cutthroat trout. Oh, yeah. Those days, carnation milk came in wooden boxes. You know, it didn't come in pasteboard like they do nowadays. You know, and we had four carnation milk boxes, five that we had. We had four to sit on and then we had one in the middle that we threw the trout in. Boy, sometimes that carnation milk box case was almost full. You know, oh, we'd recreate fried trout, I tell you. Fried trout and spuds and coffee, that's all you need for supper.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Huh?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, Pudinier, all the crates, canned stuff comes in nowadays, came in wooden boxes, don't they? What else would you guys do to pass the time besides fishing and a little hunting?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Would you sit around and... We'd go visiting down, we'd go down to Tom Howard's and we'd say that was about two and a half miles down the river. And we'd go, and we, then he got pneumonia and we'd take turns about to stay with him and keep his cabin warm and stuff and take care of him until he got over the pneumonia. And we were busy all the time, always doing something.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: You know, there used to be a phase, I don't, I suppose that's the underwater now, and it might not be either because it's pretty high. But that trail between the cabin where we stayed and Tom Howard's, there's an outcropping of rocks, they call it boom rocks. And it, it, well it, it comes like this. And then there's just a little shelf, just wide enough for a trail, and then it goes down like this so when you're out on this shelf, the river is below you, the under you. And this boom rock sticks out into the main channel.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Like, like this is the river now, boom rock sticks out.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: And I'd been down to Tom Howard to see how he was getting along, that was after he got over his pneumonia.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I stayed too long, do you know what a plucer is? It's a candle inside of a tin can, is that right?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Yeah. And all I had was a plucer. And I was going up the river and, of course, when I got out on this rock, point, the wind was so strong that it blew out my plucer. And I knew I only had one match. That's all I had. So I dropped down on my hands and knees. I didn't dare to walk, because I might have stepped off of that. But I could feel the trail with my hands, you know. And after you've passed this point, you went right into, through a bunch of nice second growth. And it was nice going. And I crawled on my hands and knees, around the point, and then I kept on a crawl until I knew I was clear in the woods, you know. And then I used my only match and lit the plucer again. And by God, I made it home. But there was just a puff of wind came, just as I hit that point. I ain't gonna, I'll tell you, I know enough to get right down on my hands and knees, right now. There wasn't no room on there at all. No. No. But you could feel the trail, you know.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: I'll never forget that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where most of these guys that, like these guys that you were living with then, guys that you knew that were lumberjacks, these other guys, they were lumberjacks, weren't they? The guys that you were staying in the cabin with?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Yeah.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Yeah. They were all young fellows. We were all young fellows. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In those days, did those guys, you guys think about getting married much? Did they talk about it? About the girls and getting married, wanting to get married? Were they looking to get married?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: No, I tell you. Not too much. The wages that you drew got those days weren't enough to get married on as a rule. Putting your, a log in camp those days were all bachelors, the whole camp. Except probably an occasional camp foreman now and then. But, S .R. Duul, they were all single men, every one of them. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what, in them days, what were the guys, what were they thinking about for the future? Did they think that they'd just keep on logging and be bachelors?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Remain bachelors?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Yeah, I guess so.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And they were living that way?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: I guess so.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Most of them liked logging enough to want to stay with it?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Well, I was probably thinking the new. Yeah.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: I went out on the coast, it was the same way.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: I'll tell you about minnie when Minnie's whorehouse came to camp.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Huh?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Mm -hmm. Well, sir, I was working in a log in camp for the Paulson Logging Company on the Grace Harbor.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: And the woods got dry. You know, when they get dry on the coast, they get dry. Right now. And they had to shut the camp down right off the bat, you know. So they were going to shut the camp down for 14 days. And this fellow, he was quite a little older than I, but he was from a hometown in the old country. His name was Gus Weston. We were going to go up to Queen's Lake and go fishing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You heard of Queen's Lake?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: I've seen it, yeah. Well, that was, they hadn't finished the highway up to it yet when we walked up there. Well, anyway, but we had to go down to Hoagam and Outfit.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Well, I have to put this story in with it. They'd had a big celebration in Saint Maries the year before. And there was a shooting gallery man there, and he gave a Remington pump rifle for the one that had the highest score that week. During this week, it was kind of a country fair, you know. Well, I tell you, there was five of us tied on 28. And then we shot off the tie and I was high. And Cap led that ran the boat from Cordal Lane to Saint Maries, came up that afternoon and shot 29.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: And the shooting gallery man called me in when I walked by. He was going to close up the next day and he told me, he said, Cap led, came up and shot 29. And he won't be here tomorrow, so I had him shoot off a tie and he shot 21 on the tie. Now I say, I tell you, George, if you can shoot 29 and if you can't beat him on the tie, I'll give you $10 out of my own pocket for the second prize. Well, I got mad and here's something that I've been trying to shoot all week, so I wouldn't have to beat those guys from the tie. And I shot 29 right off the bat. And then I just got cocky, you know, and I just banged, banged, banged and I shot 22. And I got the rifle. He said, he told me, he said, well, I don't think anybody will beat that.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: No one before you get off work, you'll find a rifle in the pool. Well, it was there. He was waiting for me. Well, when Gust and I went down to get fishing tackle and camping equipment and stuff to go up to Queen Off Lake, they were going to have a big celebration in Hoquiam . They call it the Splash. And Gust had a lot of friends in town. I was a stranger in that country, but he had a lot of friends and he told me I had to go and see these friends.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: And I walked around town. I seen a shooting gallery sign sticking out down the street and stuff like that. See, so when I came in there, there was quite a few guys right in front of there. You know where you shoot, you know, you've seen a shooting gallery. And just as I walked in through the door, somebody at the other end said, George Schmaltz in Saint Maries, Idaho.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: And I thought, Jesus Christ, what's going on here?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: And I thought, well, it's a serious right now coming in through the door. And it was the same guy that I had to shoot in the gallery in Saint Maries the year before. And he was telling these guys how I shout.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Can you beat that?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: My God.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Well, I'll tell you the, finish the story. After I cashed my time check with the Paulson Logan Company, the office I went over and shot, Wally had, that week was, you know, these great big hunting knives, like a pocket knife, folding hunting knife for each day. The man that was high each day got one of them knives. But I was, I went back and I said, I'll shoot you, I'll shoot for a dive. I didn't do very good. There was a saloon right around the corner. And I went in and I had a good swig of whiskey. I went back and I shot again. And I said, that looks pretty good, George. Well, I gave him my name and address. And my God, 14 days afterwards when I got back to camp, the hunting knife was in the former's office waiting for me. Sounds like he was running a straight game. But oh yeah, oh yeah, he was. He didn't care to have a drunk come in and spend his money. He wanted to see good shots. Oh yes. He had a Buffalo Bill haircut, you know, and a gold tee, you know. But he was a good guy. Oh, he was a good guy. You know, like in Saint Maries, he could have taken off. He says, you'll find the rifle being the pool hall after I pulled out. And he was in the pool hall. Well, what I was going to tell you though, there was two guys. One was another guy from a hometown that worked in that logan camp. Knut Vanberg was his name. And I forget to this Ed name. He was a little northern, northern Norwegian. He wasn't so little. He didn't put husky Norwegian. But Knut and Ed were going to watch camp during these 14 days. They promised the camp foreman that they were going to watch camp. But the camp foreman knew they're failing, see. So he made gusts and I promise that we'd see that Knut and Ed got back to camp, you know. Well, we got all our camping equipment and everything. And fishing tackle and stuff. And then there was what they called minis whorehouse.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Well, Gus said, now George, you're a stranger here. And they know me. You go in and get them. I'm a little on the tough side those days. So I came in and I imagined it was minis that came to the door. They asked me what I wanted. I said, I'm looking for Knut and Ed. Oh, they're upstairs with the gurs.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: They said, where are they?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Well, the first room over to the right. Okay. Well, here there's two gurs and two boys from the Sproul on top of the bed. They said, come on boys, we're going back to camp. Well, them gurs started to crush me. And I just hearded those two guys died out, you know. And the two gurs followed the boys, you know, and crying. All they had on was shoes and socks and a g -string. And here they stood on the sidewalk, kissing these bastards goodbye. Come on, come on, get in that car. We had hired a taxi to take us toward a railroad track. See, the road across the railroad track, but you had to well walk about a quarter of a mile down to camp.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Well, we got them in the car and got them on our way to camp. Everything went fine. And the camp foreman, he hired a second cook to do the cooking for these two guys. And he went with us too. So we had a little lunch that evening and we all went to bed. There was just a nice day in this bunk house and there was a... Lutheran, you know, they used to have circuit riders over the days, you know. Go to little towns and log in camps and prints, you know. And this fellow, they were Norwegians. His partner worked in camp, in this camp. But this circuit rider, they were also going up to the lake to go fishing. We were before the state in this bunk house. Well, right at the end of camp, just at the end of the bunk houses, was a high tressel over Granite Creek. And it always was real high. It always was from the tressel to the water, because I was bad at all, as high as this garage. From the tip of the roof, you know. Well, somebody had stuck a willow fishbowl with a fishline out, sticking out between the ties and hanging down into the creek, you know.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: And Gus had seen a lot of my shoot, you know, and he was going to take his 30 Remington with him. And we were just packed, but the cook was going to have lunch for all of us. So Gus said, let's see if you can cut that fishline, Jones. I got down on my name, and I got a good aim, and I missed the fishline. Well, you know, a lot of the lumberjacks had tents, you know, up on the riverbank on the other side, and the bunk houses where they lived by tools, you know. Instead of staying in a bunk house, there would be a dozen guys, you know, a lot of these guys would buy their own tents and stay in these tents. Well, here was this Ed that had hired it out of the whorehouse. He used to stand in the tent opening, and his tongue was hanging out. He said, God damn you, George. I was just going to put it in, and then you had to fire that goddamn rifle. Well, we couldn't figure out what in the hell was this. Through the night all of them in this whorehouse had walked in and had come up to camp. They hired a taxi to take them as far as the railroad truck, and then they walked down, and they were in camp.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Well, I'll tell you, I think there were four or five hookers in camp at the breakfast table that morning.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many guys were there in camp at that time?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: There were many.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: I think there was a couple guys that were going to stay in camp along with Knut and Ed. But Gus and I and the circuit driver and his partner, we didn't have anything to do with them, but we ate breakfast well. I had to laugh. There was a little red -headed whore eat right across the table from me, and she was cute as a bug she had. She was Knut's girl, I understand. She couldn't say Knut. They say Knut. Well, that's Knut in Sweden. She was talking about her darling Knut. And I started to laugh right in her face. I guess she'd had a few drinks. Knut! That man is laughing at me! Well, by God, we took off. I'll tell you, we took off. We had a good breakfast, though, when we took off. Oh, we had a day in the trip. We spent fourteen days on Quinnall's Lane. We were the circuit driver and his partner and Gus and I, we were the only campers on the Lane. There was a fellow by the name of Herb Olson, who ran a resort where you could hire boats and you could stay and have your knees there, too. But we camped out. We rented a boat from Herb Olson and then we rode up the lake toward a point. And we pulled the boat up from this point and we just made a little trail through the bushes and it was a little natural meadow. Just big enough. Just a little bigger than what we needed for the tent. Just flat as a floor. And we really had a five for two weeks.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that that happened now and then, that the whores would come into the camps?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: The only time I ever heard of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's the only time, huh?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: The only time I ever heard of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How long did the whorehouses last at Elk River? When did they disappear? Do you know?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Well, they disappeared sometime during the First World War. Because I worked out in the shipyards and when I came back, they were gone. They weren't there no more.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Sometime during the First World War.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Yeah, I think that was just gonna put it in. God damn you, George.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were those lumberjacks over on the coast around there, were they any tougher, any more, you know, more likely to get into fights and that sort of thing than they were over there?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: In a way, yes. There was, the Scandinavians and the Finns fought a lot. There's an awful Finnish community around Hoquiam and Aberdeen.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: And I think the Finns and the Scandinavians did a lot of fighting.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: There were Finns around Elk River too, weren't there?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Very few, very few. Oh, just a few. By God's attention, I don't, I have to think of any Finns around that country.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: They're generally coming batches.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: But there was a lot of them around Hoquiam and Aberdeen.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there any special reason why you preferred this country, the Elk River country, over that country, over there on the coast?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Oh, it's too wet over there. Oh, I don't like it over there. It's too wet. And I like the country with four Singsies.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Or on the coast, you might think they chose only one season. You might say two. It rains a little more in the winter than it does in the summer. But I am.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Well, you know, a lumberjack, in a lot of the reputation, the way they look at it back now, from here, looking back at it, I think the lumberjacks mixed it up quite a bit, especially when they were drinking.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: You know, fighting and knocking a guy down and stomping on him with your court boots and that kind of thing. Yeah. You know, I've never seen that. I've never seen that.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: And that used to be back East, I think, amongst the Frenchmen and those. But I never, I never backed down, but I never had any occasion to fight.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: No, no, we got along pretty good. Oh, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there a lot of ragging? You guys job each other a lot? You know, rag each other? Oh, you mean pretty jokes?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Yeah. Oh, yeah. Oh, yeah. Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: That's about the only fun we had up there.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Yeah.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: You know, we'd kid each other, you know, in a friendly way, you know, and so on, but we never...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about a guy? What about lumberjack that was crooked?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: You know, God, it was no good. A guy that you couldn't trust to take advantage of the other guys.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Weren't there some guys like that kicking around? Guys like that miner who was a jerk?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: No, no, no. See, there was no...

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: There was no chance for anything like that. You all worked, had to work and do your job, and you got your pay at the end of the month. And there was no crooked work going on.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: There weren't guys like who were trying to make their living off of gambling, you know, and who were really kind of like sharks, you know. I'll tell you, I just about came here...

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: I came in 1912, and there were guys around that tried to supplement their income with the cards. And there were some guys too that made a habit of rolling guys when they were passed out drunk. That happened too. Yeah, oh well.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: I know there was one guy in Hellscrew that... I ain't gonna mention her name. He's dead and gone now, but he's got...

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: children living there. He made a habit of rolling lumberjacks when they were drunk. Oh well.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he lumberjack himself?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: No, no.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: But we had... we had bullies.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: You know, well, I'll give you an example. I was top load on this donkey. And I guess I was the youngest man on the landing, and the top load was the boss on the landing. But everybody that worked on the landing knew that when the landing was cleaned up, they'd sit down. I didn't find a pecking job for them to do here and there. Clean up the landing and go... we had a trunk pile, you know, where dogs break off, and they got to saw that all smooth, you know. And then you hook a block to it and you have a trunk pile. Well, you can always start firing the trunk pile, you know. The men knew that when they had the landing cleaned up, they could go over to the fire by the trunk pile.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: And there's a big pull -out. He wasn't a bad guy, but he was a bully. And he could never block me. For the simple reason that the guys just worked like the dictians, because they knew that when we had the landing cleaned up, they could sit down. So all through that setting, he never had me blocked.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: All over...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Blocked? What do you mean blocked?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Well, so the landing is so filled up that you can't send a bull hook back to the woods and get more logs. You're just filled up, you know. Was he trying to...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: huh? He was trying to block you?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Yes, I think he was. Well, we had two days left for that setting. And next to the last day, there wasn't hardly any logs came down. We didn't do a darn thing that day. Not a thing. Once, I think there was one or two drags came down. And I didn't know what the hell was the matter. And then the last day, that was going to be his last day, the bull hook could go up into the woods and... Here a drag had come down and we'd unfastened him and the bull hook could go back up into the woods. And here's another drag. What he did, he yarded out all these logs the day before so he could block me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: See?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: He never did learn my name. He did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I just threw my pee, you understand?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: And he must have been watching me from trees way up in the hill. Because when he seen me start down the railroad track for camp, he came running and waving his arms. Hey boy, hey boy, wait a minute boy, hey boy. I said, fuck you, you goddamn cold out son of a bitch. And no, I never took shit from nobody those days. And you know, he never could learn my name. I just had to take my time and I let Tony... You know, the name was Tony Burch. He was a pollock. He had to clean up his own landing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was his job?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: He was the boss for the whole thing. He was hooked and he was the big shop. He was boss for the whole thing and I was boss on the landing. He under him. Yeah.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Only as a rule, the hook tender didn't have too much to say about what was going on on the landing. The hook, the top loader was boss on the landing. He never did learn. Lots of times years after he'd seen me on the... He finally got married. And he lived in Bole. And lots of times he'd seen me on the train going by, you know. Oh, hello there boy, hello boy. He never did learn my name.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why not?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: I wondered why I couldn't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I guess he just didn't like you, huh?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: No, he liked me. Oh, he did. Oh, yes, he liked me. I've seen him afterwards. He acted like he lost, like I was one of his long lost friends.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, gee, there weren't many poles in the woods, were there? Around the Upper River?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: No.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Not very many Pollacks. No. No. No, not very many Pollacks. Not a lot of Yugoslavians.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: What a seven inch.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: And... He'd always say, hello Swede. I said, hello, seven inch. I always wondered, I wondered if he's using that as a slur word. Because I'm not ashamed of being a Swede. He was kind of sticking in my craw. One day when I met him on the street, he said, hello Swede. Oh, hello, Bo Hunk. My God, you know, it was always hello George after that.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: You know.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: That's why I never got into a fight, but generally took care of it before the start.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Before it reached that point.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Yeah.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: I guess it was Ma Vine that was telling me some about the... That the Austrians in town had big bootlegs.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Yeah, there was one Austrian there. Povich was one, she said. Oh yeah, Joe Povich. He was the big bootlegger in town. Oh yeah.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Yeah.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: I wonder where he got it from.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: They brought it in by car at night.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: You think all the way from Spokane or some place like that or... Yeah, oh yeah.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: They brought it in there. And some of it was moonshine.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: There's some moonshiners down on the river Baden.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was their name now?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: I heard there was some of the soccer.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Yeah, I think there was some of the soccer too, but there was that... That's what that fella's name.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Well, it's a blank now. Don't matter.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: They get it from him too. They get it from this guy too.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Oh yeah. Oh yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Then would they sell it to all the lumberjacks no matter whether they were Swedes or whatever their nationality was?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Oh yeah. They were just big suppliers.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: The moonshiners. The Austrians and Povich and those. No, no, Povich, he bootlegged. Oh yes, he bootlegged.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Can't think of that guy's name down on the river. He moonshined. And he's also the guy that killed. There was a home fed right down... The first home fed down on the river this side of the old Dan Bridge. And a fellow by the name of Fordyce lived there. And somebody killed him, I think with an axe or something, and chopped a hole and stuck him in there, threw the ice into the river. And they never found out who'd done it. But I found out who'd done it after I came to Lewiston and worked for the railroad down here. And that's why I'm trying to think of this guy's name. There was another homesteader from up there who came down and worked on the railroad here. His name was King.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And him and this guy, what the hell is this fellow's name?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: They got drunk together up on the homestead one time. And this guy told him that he's the guy that killed Fordyce. But god damn it, I can't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: God, did he say why he'd done it?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: He thought he was squealing on him for moonshining.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did this guy from Dent supply a lot of the lumberjacks around Elk River?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Yeah. Oh yeah. Yeah, he used to bring, um, more, I guess, uh, 15, 20 gorges. Over.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: He'll come to you when you're not thinking of it, you know. That's right. That's right. That's right. Well, if you ever find out who those, there were two brothers, I think, of them. And they were, they were suppliers.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: And, uh... Oh my god, I can't think.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: I heard that some of that, some moonshine was really bad stuff. Most of it was good, but some of it was terrible. Oh yeah. Oh, some of it was terrible. Oh yeah.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Jynx. I heard about Jynx.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: J -E -N -K -S or...

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: I think so. Ira Jynx is the guy that killed four dies. Yeah. And, uh, King told me that he told him, because old King himself, he killed four dies.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Yeah, one time he, he didn't sell all of his whiskey in the, uh, Elmer Melchor Anderson camp, so he left fourteen courts with a, I think with a blacksmith to peddle it for him.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: I guess the blacksmith got drunk and they just drank up the whole world.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Well... Over here, and then around the point, you can, you can talk to each other across, you know, across the ridge, you know. The other fella, he was a clerk for years for the fire patrol, Frank Pennington was his name. He was, he had a girl out on the homestead, that him and this girl finally got married. Well, anyway, he was going out to start his homestead, and here came Tom Howard, and, of course, Tom was, he was in bootleg, and he was just going into town for drugs or something. And here were the two Jynx's. And to stop Frank and ask him if he wanted a drink.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: And, uh...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And he didn't want no drink, and when they got down to around the point, Frank could hear Tom Howard say to him, Jynx says, Didn't you know he don't drink?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Well, I didn't know he looks human. He should drink.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: I wonder if these Jynx boys came from the south, because, you know, quite a bit of that good moonshine I understood was made by folks that come from there. They came from Virginia.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: That whole outfit, and all those Snyder's and Carr's and Bonner's and stuff up above our phenom, they're all from the... Virginians. They're all Virginians. West Virginians.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: You know, I was going to ask you about the clan, the Ku Klux Klan in Elk River.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: I'll tell you, I think they were pretty strong during the First World War. But by the time I got back from Seattle from the shipyard, and of course I worked in Saint Maries, so there was no more Klan in Elk River.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: And a lot of people, you know, a lot of fanatic masons, you know, joined the Klan on account of them being anti -Catholic, you know. I know one guy, that, you know, he was very anti -Catholic.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Well, he wasn't a nation, though. He joined the Klan, I know, on the common of being against the Catholics.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Well, I heard that they'd burned some crosses around Elk River. I heard that they did, but it didn't happen while I was there. It happened while I was out in the shipyards. And then, of course, I worked in Saint Maries a while before I went back up to Elk River. And that's when they, I guess, when they burned the crosses up there. Oh.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Wasn't that something? The Klan?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Yeah. That had just about died out, I think. I hope so.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: It sounded like I could never figure out from what I heard what they were trying to do. I mean, in the South, I know they were against the Blacks, and they wanted to try to keep them down, you know.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Well, I tell you, during Reconstruction, you know, after the Civil War, I guess the carpetbaggers from up north really raised hell with the white people down south. And they had to just do something. And they just about had to start something, you know.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: But then, out here, it was, I mean, there were no Black people to speak of, so it must have been, see, I'd heard it was against Catholics, and I guess maybe that was the main thing they had going out here, was against the Catholic people.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: That'd be a little scary to see, I would think, guys walking around with sheets and burning crosses.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: You know, we were talking about Malchor Anderson before, and about his wife, and you were saying she didn't have enough sense to come in out of the rain, his wife. I was wondering, I still can't figure out, and other people have talked to me about her, I still don't understand what was so strange about her, what made her such a crazy kind of person.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was she a loose woman?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: No, no, no, no. She just didn't have any sense, she just didn't have good sense, you know. I'd say...

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Well, I'd say that she was slightly retarded, let's put it that way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was she kind of childish?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Yeah.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Well, I just wanted, for instance, if she had to go someplace, when she had a baby this time. And I forget now, what she used to... if it was turpentine or something that she'd put on this baby's vagina, she'd have smart, so she'd urinate before she left, so she wouldn't urinate in bed.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now, what do you think of that?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: That's terrible, for the kid.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: I understand that... that girl... now I heard of that, I heard here about a year ago that she's... she's grown up now, and I think she's been married, but she thinks she's complete alcoholic. She's absolutely gone.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was she a good looking woman?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Oh, oh, oh, oh.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: You know, in talking... I hear a lot about the Hobos, guys that were, you know, bumming around in the 20s and in the Depression. Oh, yeah. And I've heard too that the lumberjacks that were out of work, couldn't get jobs, could wind up having to do the same thing, themselves.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Oh, yeah. Oh, I'll tell you, after I...

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: I just quit paying the railroad fare for quite a while. You know, if I wanted to go someplace, I'd catch a freight. Yeah. Like when I worked out on the coast, like I told you, when Minnie came to camp with her girls. When I got word that they wanted me to work in a cleaning mill in Kamiah up here, I took the freight to Saint Maries. I didn't. I didn't pay no railroad fare.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: But when I got to Saint Maries, I think I bought my ticket from Saint Maries to... No, wait a minute now.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: I bought...

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: I bought my way to Saint Maries on the freight and seen him. And then we went to Spokane on the Milwaukee and then we took Dan and Pete to Kamiah. But I never paid anything between... between Hoquiam  and Saint Maries. I just rode the freight.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: It was easy to get...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: it was easy to get on and stay on?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Oh, God.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Yeah. It wasn't always easy going east. Generally going east they're loaded. But going west they're empty. You can just be afraid going west any time. But going east they're pretty loaded. Generally pretty loaded.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: There are usually a lot of guys going riding when you were on.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: You had lots of guys. Lots of time. You know, one time I worked in Saint Maries. And there was an old Swede by the name of Gus Benson, was Greenlumber Foreman. And at that time Idaho was dry. I don't know now. No. It was... I think it was local option. Benoit County was dry. That's the way it was. But you could get wished in Saltice, Montana, just over the line, you know. And nobody, all those Swede lumber pilots and stuff, they didn't know a darn thing about beating a freight. I was the only one. I had an Iso -Saltice, and a five gallon Swishke. And Gus Benson, the foreman, he had to have some of it in the rest of them. But he paid my wages all the time while I was gone. And you know, what it was like this. Here's the Lumber, here's Harry's Lumber Yard. Like so. Here's the Saint Maries River. And the railroad comes like this, around the Lumber Yard and into town. And the boarding house was over here. And the freight, as it comes along in the morning, always pulls toward the Indian, just about into the railroad yard. And the freight is strung up all along the Lumber Yard. So all I had to do was just jump out of the box car and walk through the Lumber Yard and into the boarding house, where we were staying. You know, I walked through the Lumber Yard. Nobody to stop you, nothing, you know. And there was nothing to it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What town was this by?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Saint Maries. This is right by Saint Maries.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Yeah, there. Yeah.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: The Lumber Yard, the Saint Maries River runs like so.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: And, let's see now, yeah.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: And here's the town of Saint Maries. Here's the town of Saint Maries. Here's Herrick's Lumber Yard.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: And the railroad track comes like this. And into the freight yards. Well, the boarding house where we stayed was here. But always every morning that freight would stop like so. Dead stop. And all I had to do was just jump out of the box car and walk through the Lumber Yard and into the boarding house.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: And we were to it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you be able to get up to Salties and back the next day? When you'd go?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Yeah. I took the passenger over to Salties.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, you didn't ride the freight?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: No, but I rode the freight coming back. I see. With a whiskey. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How often would you go?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Oh, whenever Gus wanted to.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: I think I made about three trips that summer while we were there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And the rest of it was for the other guys?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how was the drinking done by the men then? Would they do it just at nights when work day was over?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Oh, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, is that when they drink?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Oh, yeah.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Over weekends, generally. I was running into it mostly over weekends. Not too much on a weeknight. No, no, no. No.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: There was a couple of card sharks in Elk River one time and this same Gus Pencil, he was green -lumber -forming up at Elk River at that time. The same guy that was down for Herrick when I used to bring the whiskey from Salties. Well, we were born with a Swede family there. And here was this John. I forget his last name. He was a lumber pilot or a husky guy. And every payday, these card sharks would get ahold of him and get his paychecks. He'd always hold off if he was bored and stuff, but they'd get the rest of it. And he was a hard worker. And finally Gus Pencil, his boss, he was bored in there too. He got after John. Told him what a darn fool he was to give his money away. And John, he listened to Gus. I guess there was three, four paydays. The card sharks didn't get him. And finally one Saturday, they got him and took it all away from him. And Gus heard about it. So John, he had a room. Him and his partner had a room with his family, but Gus had a room someplace else, but he always came down for his meals. And I had a shack of my own, and I came down. The breakfast table, I think it was on a Sunday morning, Gus had found out that John had lost all his money. So he started to give him the give him. And he really talked her to him. And John, he was insane.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Anything? Anything to say?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Yes, but God damn it, Gus, it was an awful God damn good game.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What can you do with a guy like that?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Those guys really knew how to play good crooked poker, didn't they?

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: Oh, yeah. I never played. I never played. I know what I'm good at, and I don't know what I know how to do, and I don't know how to play poker. So I never played.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: I told you about Putman and the only Christian 20 minutes.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: You know, I was working with a fellow, and Gus was one of the hardest works and the biggest liars that I've ever known. His name was Putman. And Ed Lillard was the boss on the loading dock. He's retired, lived downtown here now. Well, Ed and I used to go fishing together a lot. All you had to do was just bring your pole and your basket down at noon and go up the creek a little ways after the whistle blew and get your 50 limit and go on home. There was no such a thing as, you just say, we're going to have fish for supper, and that was it. Well, you know, and Putman, he went fishing a lot too.

speaker: George Schmaltz
words: And so he brought his pole down and his basket too. And he went fishing up the creek, but we never saw hide or hair after Ed and I went up. Well, how Ed and I fished the stream, and I fished his hole, Ed and he'd go by me to the next hole and fish, and I'd go and fish the next hole. That's the way we always do.
